emails.subject: Lab Results and Updated Treatment Plan - January 8, 2026
emails.content:
Dear Amanda,

Your lab work from today is back. Here are your results:

A1C: 7.8%
Fasting glucose: 184 mg/dL

Your A1C has increased from 7.2 at your last check in June. This means your average blood sugar over the past three months has been higher than we want it to be. We discussed this today, but I wanted to put it in writing so you have it.

Here is the updated plan:
- Continue metformin 1000mg twice daily (no change in dose)
- Fasting glucose checks every 6 weeks instead of quarterly so we can track this more closely
- Focus on reducing simple carbohydrates and increasing physical activity where your knee allows
- We will recheck your A1C in 8-10 weeks

I know this is not the result you were hoping for. We will work through it. Please call the office if you have any questions.

Best,
Dr. Patel

emails.subject: Your Recent Visit Results - January 20, 2026
emails.content:
Dear Amanda,

Thank you for coming in on January 20. I wanted to follow up with your results from the examination.

Right knee assessment findings:
- Significant soft tissue inflammation around the patella and lateral aspect
- No evidence of structural damage (ACL, MCL, meniscus intact on physical exam)
- Range of motion reduced approximately 15-20% compared to normal
- Mild effusion present

This is consistent with a contusion and soft tissue injury. The good news is there does not appear to be any ligament or cartilage damage at this time.

My recommendation remains physical therapy, 2x per week for 4-6 weeks, to restore full range of motion and strengthen the surrounding muscles. I have submitted a referral to Rainier Physical Therapy. They will contact you to schedule.

In the meantime, continue with ice, compression, and ibuprofen as needed. Avoid high-impact activities until cleared.

Please don't hesitate to reach out if symptoms worsen or if you develop any new concerns.

Best,
Dr. Patel

emails.content:
Amanda,

I saw that you declined the physical therapy referral. I want to be straightforward with you. Given the nature of your injury and your physical occupation, I strongly recommend reconsidering. Without proper rehabilitation, you risk chronic instability in that knee, and the compensatory movement patterns could lead to problems in your hip and back.

Please think about it.

Best,
Dr. Patel

emails.subject: Career and Health Discussion - Follow-Up
emails.content:
Dear Amanda,

I wanted to follow up on something I raised during your visit on March 11. I know it was not easy to hear, and I wanted to give it to you in writing so you can think about it at your own pace.

Your A1C has gone from 7.2 to 8.4 in nine months. You are 42 years old. Your BMI is 33. You have a physically demanding job that keeps you on your feet 8 hours a day, often in awkward positions, with limited ability to eat regular meals or monitor your blood sugar during the workday.

I am telling you this as your doctor: the trajectory you are on is not sustainable. Uncontrolled type 2 diabetes at your age leads to neuropathy, kidney damage, and cardiovascular complications. These are not abstract risks. At 8.4 with the trend going the wrong direction, we are looking at real consequences within the next 3-5 years if we do not change course.

I strongly recommend you consider transitioning to work that is less physically demanding and allows you to manage your condition more effectively during the day. That means regular meals, the ability to check your blood sugar, and less strain on your joints.

I understand this is not a simple ask. A career change affects your family, your income, and your identity. I am not telling you to quit your job tomorrow. But I am asking you to start thinking about a plan, because the alternative is your body making the decision for you.

I am here to support you however I can. If you want to talk through this or need documentation for anything, please call the office.

Best,
Meena Patel, MD

emails.subject: Discharge Instructions: Mochi - April 14, 2026

Hi Amanda,

Mochi has been discharged following her procedure today. Here are your instructions for home care:

Procedure: Foreign body removal (hair tie)
Anesthesia: General (isoflurane)

Home Care Instructions:
- Feed soft food only for 3-5 days. Small portions, 3-4 times per day.
- No jumping or rough play for 7 days.
- Monitor for vomiting, lethargy, or loss of appetite. Call us immediately if these occur.
- Check incision site daily for swelling, redness, or discharge.
- Mochi may be groggy for 12-24 hours. This is normal.
- No food or water for 2 hours after arriving home. Then offer small amounts of water.

Medications:
- Meloxicam 0.5mg: Once daily with food for 3 days (dispensed)

Follow-up: Please schedule a follow-up visit in 7-10 days.

If you have any concerns, call us at (206) 555-7390 or the after-hours emergency line at (206) 555-7391.

Dr. Sarah Kim
Rainier Beach Animal Clinic

emails.subject: 2026 Plan Details - Prescription Drug Coverage Comparison
emails.content:
2026 Plan Comparison: Prescription Drug Coverage

HDHP with HSA:
Prescription drugs are subject to the annual deductible. You pay the full negotiated cost for medications until your deductible is met, then the plan covers 80%.
- Generic medications: full cost until deductible (avg $25-50/month)
- Preferred brand: full cost until deductible (avg $150-500/month)
- Non-preferred brand: full cost until deductible
- Specialty: full cost until deductible, then 20% coinsurance

PPO:
Prescription drug copays apply from day one. No deductible required for prescriptions.
- Generic: $15 copay
- Preferred brand: $45 copay
- Non-preferred brand: $75 copay
- Specialty: 20% coinsurance, max $250/fill

Mail-order (90-day supply) available at 2.5x copay through Express Scripts.

Full formulary lists and plan documents are available on the benefits portal.

Please complete your enrollment by December 15, 2025.

emails.subject: benefits question
emails.content: Hey Frank, quick question about the health plans. I have a couple prescriptions I fill every month and trying to figure out if the PPO or the HDHP is the smarter move. Do you know if most people go PPO when they have regular meds?

emails.subject: Re: benefits question
emails.content: PPO has flat copays for scripts. HDHP you pay full price until the deductible. If you have regular prescriptions go PPO.

emails.subject: Re: benefits question
emails.content: Thanks Frank, that's what I was leaning toward. Appreciate it.
